title: Google India Redesign
subtitle: Streamlining search and enhancing discoverability for India's diverse web users.
problem_statement: The current Google India homepage, while visually clean, presents legacy UI elements like the "I'm Feeling Lucky" button that add cognitive load without clear modern utility. Important dynamic information, such as the "Ready, set, I/O" announcement, is relegated to small text, easily overlooked. Furthermore, the "Google offered in:" language options are visually deemphasized at the bottom, creating friction for users seeking immediate language customization in a multilingual market.

pillars.title: Cognitive Load
pillars.insight: The presence of two search buttons ("Google Search", "I'm Feeling Lucky") directly below the search bar, particularly the less-used "I'm Feeling Lucky", forces a micro-decision that adds unnecessary cognitive effort for the primary task.

pillars.title: Information Scent
pillars.insight: The "Ready, set, I/O" announcement is presented as small, plain text, making it easy for users to overlook critical, time-sensitive information, reducing its impact and visibility.

pillars.title: Cultural Relevance
pillars.insight: The "Google offered in:" language options are visually small and positioned at the bottom of the page, indicating a potential friction point for users in India who might prefer to switch languages immediately upon arrival.

artifacts.label: Strategy
artifacts.title: Defining the Challenges
questions: How might we reduce cognitive load by re-evaluating the utility and prominence of legacy buttons like "I'm Feeling Lucky"?
How might we make timely announcements, such as "Ready, set, I/O", more visible and engaging without cluttering the minimalist interface?
How might we enhance the discoverability and ease of use for regional language selection, currently presented as small text at the bottom?

personas.name: Rohan
personas.quote: Just get me the information, fast.
personas.bio: Rohan uses Google daily for work and personal queries. He values speed and directness. He finds the "I'm Feeling Lucky" button an unnecessary visual element that he never uses. He might miss important announcements if they aren't prominent.

personas.name: Priya
personas.quote: I want to find things in my language easily.
personas.bio: Priya is comfortable with technology but often prefers to browse and search in Hindi or other regional languages. She finds the small "Google offered in:" text at the bottom of the page inconvenient and wishes for a more prominent language selection option. She might be interested in local news or events that could be announced more clearly.

points.action: Replace "I'm Feeling Lucky" button with a "Discover" button that leads to a personalized news/content feed.
points.benefit: Reduces cognitive load for primary search, repurposes legacy space for modern content discovery, and adds value beyond basic search.

points.action: Integrate the "Google offered in:" language options into a more prominent dropdown menu near the search bar or top right, combining it with existing settings/account options.
points.benefit: Enhances accessibility and discoverability of language switching, streamlining the localization process for users.

points.action: Adapt the "Ready, set, I/O" announcement into a dismissible, visually distinct banner or notification bar above the search field.
points.benefit: Ensures critical, time-sensitive information is highly visible without permanently cluttering the minimalist interface, improving user awareness.

points.action: Modify the search bar itself to include a dynamic 'trending searches' or 'quick access' icon that expands on hover/click.
points.benefit: Offers proactive suggestions and quick access to common tools without disrupting the initial clean view, enhancing efficiency and discoverability.

pivot.before: The current Google India homepage relies on extreme minimalism, which inadvertently hides important dynamic information ("Ready, set, I/O") and makes crucial localization options ("Google offered in:") difficult to discover, while retaining a low-utility legacy button ("I'm Feeling Lucky"). This leads to missed engagement opportunities and unnecessary cognitive load.
pivot.pivot_logic: Shift from a purely reactive, minimalist search portal to a subtly proactive and contextually aware entry point that intelligently surfaces relevant information and empowers users with easily accessible customization, particularly for language, without increasing visual clutter. The focus is on reducing cognitive friction and enhancing discoverability.
pivot.after: A refined homepage that maintains core search efficiency, integrates dynamic announcements more prominently yet dismissibly, offers intuitive and visible language selection, and repurposes legacy elements for modern, personalized discovery. This reduces cognitive load, improves user engagement, and better serves the diverse needs of the Indian market.

stages.actions: User lands on page, scans for search bar.
stages.friction: Briefly notices "I'm Feeling Lucky" button, but ignores it as irrelevant.

stages.touchpoints: Search bar with magnifying glass and microphone icons
stages.actions: User types keywords into the search bar.

stages.friction: Momentary decision between "Google Search" and "I'm Feeling Lucky" buttons, adding a slight cognitive pause.

stages.actions: User scans search results for relevant information.
stages.friction: Not applicable to the provided screenshot.

stages.actions: User scans bottom of page, clicks small text to change language.
stages.friction: Small text, low visibility, requires deliberate scanning and scrolling to find language options.

annotations.title: Repurposed 'I'm Feeling Lucky' Area
annotations.rationale: The 'I'm Feeling Lucky' button (approx. x=500, y=400) currently offers low utility for most users. This prominent space can be repurposed for a 'Discover' button or a personalized content feed toggle, reducing cognitive load by removing an unnecessary choice and adding value.

annotations.title: Enhanced Language Selection
annotations.rationale: The 'Google offered in:' text (approx. x=400, y=550) is small and at the bottom, making language switching difficult. Elevating this to a prominent dropdown near the search bar or top right will improve accessibility and user control for India's multilingual users.

annotations.title: Prominent Announcement Display
annotations.rationale: The 'Ready, set, I/O' announcement (approx. x=350, y=500) is easily missed due to its small text size and placement. Implementing a dismissible banner or notification bar for such critical updates will ensure visibility without permanent clutter, improving information scent.
